title: The Fading Line
passage:
Ask a driver how long the white paint on a motorway lasts and the guess is usually years. On the busiest lanes, the highways engineer Ivar Krull found, the honest answer can be a single winter. The wear, though, is not what most people picture. A tyre almost never passes directly over the painted line; cars straddle it, one wheel to each side. What actually grinds a marking away is the grit and road salt that passing traffic drags across it and, on northern roads, the steel edge of the snowplough. That is why the same paint behaves so strangely from place to place. On a quiet country lane, spared both heavy traffic and the plough, a line laid down in one decade may still be serviceable well into the next, while its motorway cousin is repainted three or four times a year.
— Sören Vall, transport notebook

questions.prompt: According to the text, what mainly determines how long a road marking stays serviceable?

options.text: The grit and salt that traffic drags over the line, together with snowplough blades, rather than its age or tyres running over it.
The steady passage of tyres directly over the painted line, which slowly grinds the paint down on busy roads.
Years of exposure to sunlight and rain, which gradually break the paint down wherever a marking is laid.
The grade of paint chosen, since cheaper, shorter-lived paint is used on busy motorways to hold down costs.

questions.rationale: TYPE-001 direct detail (paraphrase of the cause the passage names). The text says 'What actually grinds a marking away is the grit and road salt that passing traffic drags across it and, on northern roads, the steel edge of the snowplough', and attributes the place-to-place difference to how much traffic and ploughing a line meets, not to its age; A paraphrases exactly that. B = contradiction/too_literal: it names the intuitive cause the passage explicitly overturns — 'A tyre almost never passes directly over the painted line; cars straddle it'. C = outside_knowledge: weathering by sun and rain is a plausible real-world guess but is never stated; the text pins wear on grit, salt, and the plough. D = outside_knowledge: the passage says nothing about different grades of paint or cost-driven paint choices — it ties lifespan to abrasion, not paint quality.

generator_meta.topic: the surprising lifespan of painted road markings — abrasion from traffic-borne grit and snowploughs, not tyres or age, governs how long a line lasts

fictional_source.byline: Sören Vall, invented transport journalist; finding attributed to invented highways engineer Ivar Krull
fictional_source.publication_type: popular-science and infrastructure monthly (BrE house style)

generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('tyre' not 'tire'; 'snowplough' not 'snowplow'; no AmE-only spellings present).
generator_meta.originality_note: Ivar Krull and Sören Vall are invented; the grit/salt/snowplough abrasion mechanism is generic engineering colour, not a quotation of any real study, person, or publication. No famous-thesis anchoring.
